title: Lesson 8: The Grace Cycle to Forgiveness
description: Four words explain what God did for you at the cross: Grace, Mercy, Justified, and Righteous. Through an interactive diagram you will build step by step, you will see how these four realities work together in a cycle that starts with God's love and flows through you to others.

blocks.text: In the last lesson, you traced the one storyline that holds the entire Bible together, from creation to new creation. You saw that every page points to Jesus. Now we are going to zoom in on what Jesus actually accomplished for you at the cross.

For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast. For we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand, that we should walk in them.

Paul says three things that will reshape how you understand everything God has done for you. First, salvation is a gift. You did not earn it. Second, it came by grace through faith, not by anything you did. Third, God did not save you so you could sit still. He saved you so that His grace would flow through you and into the lives of the people around you.

This lesson is built around four words that every believer should know: Grace, Mercy, Justified, and Righteous. These are big church words, but they describe something so simple and so good that once you see it, you will never forget it.
Four Words That Change Everything

Grace means getting a gift you do not deserve. You did nothing to earn it, and you could never pay it back. Mercy means not getting the punishment you do deserve. You broke the law, you earned the sentence, but the judge does not punish you. Justified means your punishment has been paid. Someone else stepped in and settled your debt. Righteous means you are now seen as perfect before God, not because you became perfect overnight, but because Jesus' perfect record was placed on your account.
Put those four together and you have the whole story of what happened to you at the cross. But before we open the diagram, picture it like this.
The Boy and the Fruit Tree

A father had a favorite fruit tree in his backyard and told his sons not to play in it. The younger son did not listen. He snuck out, climbed the tree, and when he climbed down, he broke half the branches, and the tree was ruined. The father walked outside, looked at the damage, and said, "Somebody is going to pay for this." The boy's older brother heard what happened. Without being asked, he emptied his entire savings account and bought his father a brand-new tree to replace the ruined one. Then the father took the boys to the amusement park.
Grace. Mercy. Justified. Righteous. All four are in this story. After you walk through the steps in the diagram below, we will use this story to help us understand these terms.
Walking Through the Five Steps

Step 1: Mercy. In the story of the boy, the Father does not punish the young boy for ruining the tree. In the same way, God looked at your sin and had every right to condemn you. Instead, He responded with compassion. Mercy is not getting the punishment you do deserve.

Step 2: Justified. We saw how the older brother pays for the ruined tree. The cost falls entirely on him, not the boy. Jesus paid for the cost of our sin and makes God's mercy just. Justified means your punishment is paid in full, and it is "Just-if-I'd" never sinned.

Step 3: Righteous. The Father now has a new tree, and the relationship with the boy is all-good. That is righteousness. God did not just erase your old record, but He replaced it with a new one. The perfect life that Jesus lived is the very life that God sees when He looks at us. The "Great Exchange" is Jesus takes the punishment for our sin and gives us His perfection.
For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.
Step 4: Grace. Lastly, the father gives the boy a gift. He takes him to the amusement park. The boy who broke everything gets a reward he never earned. That is grace. We get a gift we do not deserve.
For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast.

Step 5: The Grace Cycle. The hope is the boy remembers the grace that he has received and then often gives mercy and grace to others. We are created to do good works overflowing from the love God has given to us. This is how the cycle works. Look back at the diagram: when the arrow reaches Grace, it does not stop. It points toward Mercy. Those who have received mercy become people who show mercy. Those who have been given grace become people who give grace.
Be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you.
8.1 - How has this story of the boy or The Grace Cycle helped you understand what Jesus has done for you?

8.2 - Think about the fruit tree story. Have you been living more like the boy who thinks he still owes a debt, or like the boy whose brother already paid it? What would change if you really believed the debt was settled?
8.3 - Who in your life needs you to show them the mercy or grace you have received from God? What would that look like this week?
The Fruit Tree in the Garden

Let's be reminded that the Bible begins with a boy and a tree. In Genesis 3, Adam reached for the fruit of a tree he was told not to touch, and he broke everything. Sin, shame, and death entered the world because of what happened at that tree. In the righteous justice of God, all sin must be rightly paid for. In John 3:16 we see "For God so loved the world that He gave..." Jesus. He stretched out His arms on another tree and paid for our sin. One tree broke the world. Another tree (the cross) healed it. And now the Father delights to give His good gifts to everyone who puts their faith in Jesus. We are God's workmanship to do good works, share His grace and mercy, and live a life of forgiveness. That is the grace cycle.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson, and what next step can you take this week?
What have you learned about God and yourself recently in your Daily Devotions?
What is a prayer request for this week?
